**Alexander the Great Regatta (Pre-Regatta)**
Day 1
Shifty 10-18kts from 300-340º we sailed mostly on Course A which is sort of on the corner of the Thessaloniki Yacht Club area.  There were a few container ships anchored upwind of us on the other side of the channel.  It was a day of shifty winds and unpredictable puffs.

Strategy
The wind from the right would often wave through the course, moving quickly.  Whereas, the wind from the right would sit off of the course above the port layline and really only reward those who went all the way out there.  There were shifts 

Day 2
Unstable sea breeze 200-220º 5-10kts.  Was a mostly right hand biased track with opportunities across the middle at time.  We were on course B which is slightly more East of the point off of the Yacht Club.  End of day big right shift.
Ended up -2 on starboard and base on the port lower for the final race.
Kite timing on gybe is critical. Need to have luff working on new side by DDW.

**Training**
Gybing in light winds
- Pop the battens with body more to windward, windward foot on wing, more leverage
- Pump/flatten more forward on the wing (canoe mode) to keep kite nicely engaged
- Rotate kite super early if possible especially when doing a lay down gybe (mega-light)

Starting in light winds
- Focus more on crunching up on the boat to windward, rather than rolling the boat to leeward
- The boat to leeward will take care of itself once you have establishing your lane later on in light winds, can't force the roll
- Look for a late maneuver to windward
- Engage foils and flow (almost race mode) when taking a big double tack on port
- Mega-light winds is usually Ian weight on flatten, as I will have mainsail work to keep on

Upwind technique in Greece (flat water / light air)
- More steady boat trim
- Lead with the mainsail to make mode change moves, since there is nothing to slow the boat down like chop doing the small body pumps does not seem as effective and rather it might mess with the flow

Story: 230-240 seems to be safety right (almost start and tack). As the wind backs, the story gets weird and potentially a pressure bonus course left (not congruent with DW gains)
In boat:
-Communication on launch (good and bad moments!)
-Missing moments to utilize the bow in around the course
      If I’m tracking the race well, these shouldn’t be a surprise… 
-Ian comms when finding the mode in changing conditions (R2 L1?) 


- Wind left of 210º watch the left, wind right of 215º watch the right
- Course is almost always open, though.  Very unstable these light seabreezes
	- Stay alert and always be willing to reevaluate a new plan even when it all seems obvious
	- I have gotten a bit too comfortable when things look like they will be all good and simple and missed a new change
- Stay positive, stay positive, next move, next move

Seemed like there was some sort of current or flow making it hard to stay on the line for the entirety of the pre start. The pin waiting position is about a boat length further away from the pin than I seem to expect.  

-comms when finding VMG around the course

Day 3 Notes (Klaus)
- Stay aware of wind condition to make changes in the set up
- Settings
	- Working the wing (-2+1)
	- Working into the hull (-4+2)
	- Double trap (base)
- Gybing when we are both trapezing, don’t kill the heeling when popping the battens cause then we only have to wait to develop to double trap
- Better to pop just the two from bellow and sync to hit together
- “High mode” acceleration to lock the boat as soon as possible with the main

Base seems to be 6 kts+… working on finding a half minus.

Day 4 Notes
- Make sure we are all the way to the pressure on our side before we tack early or gybe early.
- Downwind moding
	- When developing a higher faster mode in lighter airs:
	- Keep Ian forward on the skate grip part of front wing
	- Work my weight aft and then out to edge of wing
	- Once developed on the edge of wing try to stay there by doing some micro driving to keep my weight up
- Upwind I would say we are still missing our higher/faster mode (ofcouse would be nice).  Ideas we are trying in lighter airs:
	- An  in-between setting between base and the full drifter set up. (-2+1). I think this feels nice and is a bit forgiving. 
	- Jib set up still elusive.  Kiwis going middle hole and higher on jib and really nice high mode, maybe a slightly flatter twister jib is better?  Maybe the top hole is just too deep in some parts for how light and flat water it is.

URU mode DW 

Ian needs to utilize shoulders more upwind to keep the boat tracking 

Would love to work on tight laylines up wind (doubles at the pin) to iron out the moves with a last minute shoot.

Day 5 Notes
Seems like a gain to be taking our *slightly flatter* setup higher in the wind range.  Gives us more range on trimming and movement I think.  Will this work in more seastate?  I am not sure.
Need to develop a batten tuning system in Long Beach.  How do we measure and when do we change?

When gybing in super light air pop from further forward on wing as it gives nice depth to the spinnaker for some reason.
We started to doing tacks where Nevin takes main more, no drama, but what is our overall thinking here?  Happy with the change or should we find ways to make the Ian pass the main style easier?

Medal Race

Challenging sea state outside of the break wall. One set of waves was offset left of the TWD maybe ~200*. With the wave offset, any header on starboard was really easy to keep the speed in th waves.

Raced 3 one lappers with CHN. Strong course left, but boat was a handful with both the main and the jib very deep and powerful. Need to read the Hyeres notes to refresh our technique and tuning. The obvious one is looser lowers…

Sailed solo with looser lowers than previous day. This put us close to the regular tuning guide (12mm PB @22 tension) and the boat was much easier to sail on VMG. Big difference was the frequency that the boat picked up to +2 heel. The boat goes well when puff+lifts pick the boat up a bit, but nothing more.

Possibly quicker to depower the main on the whole and board up to accompany that? I think jib and board charts we need to keep working on. 

Technique tips: Ian needs to trim the boat to keep the boat unloaded consistently. The obvious here seems to be a quicker ease.    The Austrians go quite well and a major difference is the main trimming style. This should come together with a setup that is “easy” to sail (flat and twisty). One critical thing to figure out is what actually accelerated the boat in this mode? If the main is very twist there shouldn’t need to be a whole lot of mainsheet ease that leads to acceleration. The ease should keep the boat tracking correctly, so Ian needs to work on giving “pressing” calls before the boat actually slows down. Since the main is twisty, a small ease can accompany a rudder press and the boat should accelerate. If we are already on angle I would assume a jib reset when the puff comes on is a big tool to keep the boat tracking on the line.
